question: Design a Parking Lot System that supports multiple floors, different vehicle types (Car, Truck, Motorcycle), multiple spot types (Compact, Large, Handicapped, Electric), automated ticket generation, hourly-rate payment, and real-time spot tracking.

walkthrough.title: Define Enums for Type Safety
walkthrough.description: Start by creating enums for VehicleType, SpotType, TicketStatus, and PaymentMethod. Enums prevent invalid states and make the code self-documenting.

walkthrough.title: Abstract Vehicle Class with Spot Compatibility
walkthrough.description: Create an abstract Vehicle base class with a canFitInSpot(SpotType) method. Each subclass (Car, Truck, Motorcycle) defines which spot types it can occupy — this is the Template Method pattern.

walkthrough.title: ParkingSpot with Thread-Safe Operations
walkthrough.description: assignVehicle() and removeVehicle() are synchronized to handle concurrent access. The spot validates vehicle compatibility before assignment.

walkthrough.title: ParkingFloor with Spot Search
walkthrough.description: Each floor maintains a list of spots. findAvailableSpot() uses Java Streams to find the first available compatible spot — can be optimized with a priority queue for large lots.

walkthrough.title: Ticket Generation with UUID
walkthrough.description: Tickets are auto-generated with a unique ID (TKT-XXXXXXXX) using UUID.randomUUID(). Duration is calculated by rounding up to the nearest hour.

walkthrough.title: Strategy Pattern for Pricing
walkthrough.description: The PricingStrategy interface allows pluggable pricing models. HourlyPricingStrategy maps each vehicle type to an hourly rate — adding weekend/surge pricing requires zero changes to ParkingLot.

walkthrough.title: Singleton ParkingLot with Double-Check Locking
walkthrough.description: The ParkingLot class uses the double-checked locking singleton pattern with volatile keyword for thread safety. It orchestrates the full park/unpark flow.

followUpQuestions.question: How would you handle electric vehicle charging spots?
followUpQuestions.answer: Add an ELECTRIC SpotType and a ChargingSession class that tracks kWh consumed. The PricingStrategy can be extended to include charging costs. The ParkingSpot gets a hasCharger flag and optionally a ChargingStation reference.

followUpQuestions.question: How do you handle concurrent entry at multiple gates?
followUpQuestions.answer: The parkVehicle() method is synchronized on the ParkingLot instance. For higher throughput, use a ReentrantLock per floor or a ConcurrentLinkedQueue of available spots per type, allowing lock-free spot allocation.

followUpQuestions.question: How would you add surge pricing during peak hours?
followUpQuestions.answer: Create a new SurgePricingStrategy that implements PricingStrategy. It checks the current time and applies a multiplier during peak hours. Use the Decorator Pattern to wrap the base HourlyPricingStrategy with surge logic.

followUpQuestions.question: What if the parking lot needs to support reservations?
followUpQuestions.answer: Add a Reservation class with startTime, endTime, and spotId. Modify findAvailableSpot() to exclude reserved spots for the requested time window. Use a ScheduledExecutorService to auto-release expired reservations.

followUpQuestions.question: How would you persist the data?
followUpQuestions.answer: Use a relational database with tables: parking_spots, vehicles, tickets, payments. The ParkingLot singleton becomes a service layer that delegates to a ParkingRepository (DAO pattern). Use connection pooling (HikariCP) for performance.
